nativeRange.habitat: Garden Eels inhabit sandy slopes and open sandy areas adjacent to coral reefs, at depths ranging from 1 to 50 metres. They tend to congregate in areas with gentle to moderate water current.

decorPreferences: deep sand bed (20–30 cm fine sand)

diet.notes: Feeds predominantly on zooplankton drifting in the water column. In aquaria, has been documented accepting live brine shrimp (Artemia), mysis shrimp, copepods, amphipods, and finely chopped seafood delivered by gentle current. Generally requires target feeding two to three times daily; an automatic feeder with a circulation pump is commonly recommended to simulate continuous plankton drift. Live prey is often necessary during initial acclimation.

summary: The Garden Eel (Heteroconger hassi) lives permanently anchored in a self-made burrow in the sand, extending its body to feed on passing plankton. It is widely considered expert-level due to specialized feeding requirements and sensitivity to disturbance.
careNotes:
Garden Eels are among the more challenging saltwater fish to maintain long-term. They spend virtually their entire lives with their posterior buried in a self-made burrow of at least 20–30 cm of fine sand; they tend to retreat fully when alarmed. A tank of at least 125 gallons (473 L) with a deep, unobstructed sand bed is generally advised, and each eel has been reported to need roughly 30 × 30 cm of personal sand territory. Groups of 3–10 individuals are commonly recommended, as solitary specimens tend to refuse food and decline. Male conspecifics have been known to be territorial toward one another, particularly during spawning activity, so adequate spacing is important.

Feeding is the primary challenge: these fish are obligate water-column planktivores and have been reported to reject non-moving food. A circulation pump or automatic feeder delivering small frozen or live particles (brine shrimp, mysis, copepods) past their burrows is widely considered essential. Acclimation to frozen foods can take weeks of patience with live Artemia.

Reef-safe rating is listed as 'with caution' because, while they ignore corals entirely, their diet of small zooplankton means they may occasionally consume very small ornamental crustaceans. Fast-swimming or boisterous tank mates cause chronic stress, which leads to refusal to feed.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has been reported as extremely rare. In the wild, males and females emerge partially from adjacent burrows to mate. Larvae are pelagic and require separate rearing tanks with targeted zooplankton cultures. No established captive-breeding protocol is widely documented in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Individual product page confirmed 125-gallon minimum, carnivore diet, expert care level, and 26-inch adult size. Scientific name given as Taenioconger hassi (synonym for Heteroconger hassi per FishBase).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Congridae / Anguilliformes), max size (45 cm), native range (Indo-Pacific, Red Sea to Society Islands), temperature (25–29°C), and diet (zooplankton). Confirmed accepted scientific name as Heteroconger hassi.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–80°F = 22.2–26.7°C), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), dKH (8–12), adult size (16 in = 40.6 cm), tank size (125 gal), care level (expert). Used for averaging high-stakes fields.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (16 in = 40.6 cm), tank size (125–180 gal), diet details, and lifespan (35–40 years — single-source claim, treated with caution). Confirmed reef-safe and expert care level.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (22–26°C), salinity (SG 1.022–1.025), pH (8.0–8.4), adult size (40 cm), care level (moderate-to-easy — outlier vs. other sources; averaged to expert). Confirmed family Congridae and order Anguilliformes.

fish.breedingNotes: No established captive-breeding protocol exists for this species. Larvae are pelagic and would require separate rearing infrastructure to survive. Breeding in aquaria has been reported as extremely rare.
